title: Mercury
text: Mercury is the smallest planet in the Solar System and the closest to the Sun. It has an orbital period of 88 Earth days and a diameter of 4879 km. Mercury has no atmosphere to speak of and no natural satellites. Surface temperatures swing from -173°C at night to 427°C during the day. Mercury was first flown by Mariner 10 in 1974.

title: Venus
text: Venus is the second planet from the Sun and the hottest planet in the Solar System, with a mean surface temperature of 464°C. Its dense atmosphere of carbon dioxide and sulfuric-acid clouds creates a runaway greenhouse effect. Venus has a diameter of 12104 km and an orbital period of 225 Earth days. It rotates backward relative to most planets and has no moons.

title: Earth
text: Earth is the third planet from the Sun and the only known planet to harbor life. Its orbital period is 365.25 days and its diameter is 12742 km. Earth has one natural satellite, the Moon, whose diameter is 3474 km. About 71% of Earth's surface is covered by water. Its atmosphere is approximately 78% nitrogen and 21% oxygen.

title: Mars
text: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun, known as the Red Planet due to iron-oxide dust on its surface. It has an orbital period of 687 Earth days, a diameter of 6779 km, and two small moons named Phobos and Deimos. Mars is home to Olympus Mons, the largest volcano in the Solar System, rising 21.9 km above its base. NASA's Perseverance rover landed in Jezero Crater in 2021.

title: Jupiter
text: Jupiter is the fifth planet from the Sun and the largest in the Solar System, with a diameter of 139820 km — more than 11 times Earth's. Its orbital period is 11.86 Earth years. Jupiter has 95 known moons as of 2023, including the four Galilean moons: Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto. The Great Red Spot is a persistent anticyclonic storm that has been observed for at least 350 years.

title: Saturn
text: Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun and the second largest, famous for its prominent ring system composed mostly of ice particles. Its diameter is 116460 km and its orbital period is 29.46 Earth years. Saturn has 146 known moons, with Titan being the largest — and the only moon in the Solar System with a substantial atmosphere, composed mostly of nitrogen.

title: Uranus
text: Uranus is the seventh planet from the Sun and rotates on its side, with an axial tilt of 97.77°. Its diameter is 50724 km and it completes an orbit every 84 Earth years. Uranus has 27 known moons named mostly after Shakespearean characters (e.g., Miranda, Ariel, Umbriel, Titania, Oberon). It was discovered by William Herschel in 1781.

title: Neptune
text: Neptune is the eighth and outermost planet from the Sun. Its diameter is 49244 km and its orbital period is 164.8 Earth years. Neptune was discovered in 1846 based on mathematical predictions by Urbain Le Verrier. It has 14 known moons; Triton is the largest and orbits retrograde. Winds on Neptune can reach 2100 km/h, the fastest in the Solar System.

title: Pluto
text: Pluto is a dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt, reclassified from a full planet by the International Astronomical Union in 2006. Its diameter is 2376 km, and its orbital period is 248 Earth years. Pluto has five known moons; the largest, Charon, is about half Pluto's size. NASA's New Horizons probe flew by Pluto in 2015.

title: Earth's Moon
text: Earth's Moon is the fifth largest moon in the Solar System, with a diameter of 3474 km. It orbits Earth every 27.3 days at an average distance of 384400 km. The Moon's gravity is about one-sixth of Earth's. It has no substantial atmosphere. The first human landing was Apollo 11 in July 1969; twelve astronauts walked on its surface between 1969 and 1972.

title: Io
text: Io is the innermost of Jupiter's four Galilean moons and the most volcanically active body in the Solar System, with over 400 active volcanoes. Its diameter is 3643 km. Io was discovered by Galileo Galilei in 1610. Its surface is covered in sulfur and sulfur dioxide frost, giving it a distinctive yellow-orange appearance.

title: Europa
text: Europa is the second of Jupiter's Galilean moons, with a diameter of 3122 km. It has a smooth icy surface covering a subsurface liquid-water ocean that may contain more water than all of Earth's oceans combined. Europa was discovered by Galileo Galilei in 1610. It is considered one of the most promising places to search for extraterrestrial life.
